items.q: Why do specialised cells differ if they share the same DNA?
items.correct: All body cells carry the same genes, but different genes are switched on or off, so each cell makes different proteins.
items.misconception: Specialised cells differ because they contain different genes.
items.random_wrong: Specialised cells lose the genes they don't need.
items.why: Students think differentiation means losing or gaining genes rather than regulating gene expression.

items.q: How many chromosomes do gametes have compared with body cells?
items.correct: Gametes are haploid, with half the chromosome number of diploid body cells, restored to diploid at fertilisation.
items.misconception: Gametes have the same number of chromosomes as body cells.
items.random_wrong: Gametes have twice as many chromosomes as body cells.
items.why: Students forget that meiosis halves the chromosome number before fertilisation.

items.q: What can a totipotent stem cell do?
items.correct: It can divide and differentiate into any cell type, including the extra-embryonic tissues such as the placenta.
items.misconception: A totipotent cell can only become one specific type of cell.
items.why: Students confuse 'toti-' (all) with a limited or fixed potential.

items.q: Gametes are produced by which type of cell division?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: meiosis. “mitosis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A cell becomes specialised mainly by:
items.correct: switching particular genes on or off
items.misconception: losing the genes it does not need
items.why: The answer examiners want is: switching particular genes on or off. “losing the genes it does not need” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A totipotent stem cell is one that can:
items.correct: develop into any cell type, including extra-embryonic tissue
items.misconception: only become a red blood cell
items.why: It's develop into any cell type, including extra-embryonic tissue. “only become a red blood cell” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The acrosome reaction allows a sperm to:
items.correct: digest through the egg's outer layers
items.why: Correct answer: digest through the egg's outer layers. “swim faster towards the egg” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
